lesson.menu_title: A Taste of Place -- Food, Culture and How Things Are Made
lesson.menu_desc: Reading and talking about food and how it is made, with the passive voice -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- A Taste of Place
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 12 -- A Taste of Place

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a basic food that is eaten very regularly
a rare or luxurious food that is highly valued
something you only enjoy after trying it a few times
a dish a place is especially famous for
salty or spicy rather than sweet
to cook gently in liquid, just below boiling

reading.rtitle: The Dish That Tells a Story
paras: Every country has a dish that is loved by almost everyone who grows up there. It is rarely the fancy food that is served in expensive restaurants. More often it is a humble staple, something hearty that is cooked at home and passed down from one generation to the next. Ask people about it and their eyes light up, because the recipe is tied to a grandmother, a village, a season. A place is not only seen; it is tasted.

Take Norwegian brown cheese. The whey is simmered slowly for hours until the sugars caramelize and it turns a deep golden brown. It is then pressed into blocks and left to cool. For a first-timer it is very much an acquired taste, sweet and savory at the same time, but for Norwegians it is a beloved specialty, and it is eaten on thick slices of bread almost every morning. The ingredients are locally sourced, the method has barely changed in two hundred years, and the whole country is quietly renowned for it.

This is why food is such a good way into a culture. When a dish is explained to you, a little of its history is explained too: what is grown nearby, what has been traded for centuries, what is celebrated and when. You do not need to speak the language perfectly. If you are curious, and you ask how something is made, you will be told far more than a recipe. You will be handed a story.
reading.source: Adapted from a food-writing essay, A Taste of Place

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The best food in the world is served in expensive restaurants.
A place's everyday, home-cooked food carries its history, and asking how a dish is made opens a door into the culture.
Norwegian brown cheese is the only food worth trying when you travel.

items: The writer says the dish that people love most is usually fancy restaurant food.
He says it is rarely the fancy food served in restaurants, and more often a humble staple cooked at home.
Norwegian brown cheese is made by simmering the whey until the sugars caramelize.
The text says the whey is simmered slowly for hours until the sugars caramelize and it turns golden brown.
The writer says brown cheese is easy to love on the first try.
He says for a first-timer it is very much an acquired taste.
According to the writer, asking how a dish is made teaches you about the culture.
He says when a dish is explained, a little of its history is explained too, and you are handed a story.

parts: for hours until it caramelizes. For a first-timer it is an

gapfill.text: This whole text uses the PASSIVE VOICE. We use it when the action matters more than who does it: 'The whey is simmered' (we do not care who simmers it), 'it is eaten on bread', 'the ingredients are locally sourced'. Form: be + PAST PARTICIPLE. Present: is/are made. Past: was invented, were grown. Present perfect: has been traded for centuries. Add 'by + agent' only when it matters: 'a dish loved by everyone'.

bank.label: Useful language for describing food and how it is made
items: It is made from just a few simple ingredients.
The whey is simmered for hours.
It is served with thick slices of bread.
This dish is eaten almost every morning.
The country is renowned for it.
The recipe has been passed down for generations.
It is very much an acquired taste.

lf.title: Active or passive? (blue = the passive: be + past participle)

lf.text: The active sentences (cook, eat) put the person first: we know and care who does it. The blue passives (is cooked, is simmered) put the food first, because the action matters more than the cook. Form: be + past participle. Use the passive to describe processes, recipes and traditions, where 'who' is obvious or unimportant.

items: Describe a typical Brazilian dish to a foreign friend. Explain how it is made, using the passive (it is made from..., it is served with...).
Talk about a food you tried abroad that was an acquired taste. Say how it is eaten and what it is served with.
A visitor asks what your city or region is renowned for. Answer with at least two passive sentences.
Explain a family recipe that has been passed down. Describe the steps in the passive, then say why it matters to you.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = specialty, 2 = simmered, 3 = acquired taste, 4 = locally sourced, 5 = renowned for, 6 = is eaten.
Model (present passive): 'Feijoada is made from black beans and pork, and it is served with rice, orange and greens.'
Model (process): 'The beans are soaked overnight, then they are simmered for hours until they are soft.'
Structure: passive = be + past participle. Present: is/are made. Past: was invented. Present perfect: has been passed down. Add 'by + agent' only when it matters.
answerkey.note: Do not drop the verb 'be': not 'This dish served with rice' but 'This dish is served with rice'. And use the past participle: 'is made' (not 'is make'), 'was eaten' (not 'was eat').

listenings.text: Hi Marcelo, it is Linnea. I promised I would tell you how my grandmother's fish soup is made, so here it is, before I forget. First, the fish heads and bones are simmered very slowly for a whole afternoon, until the broth turns rich and golden. Nothing is wasted, that is the whole point. Then the vegetables are added, always whatever is in season, and a little cream at the end. It is served with dark bread and a lot of talking. It is not a fancy dish, it is not a delicacy, but it is loved by everyone in my family, and the recipe has never been written down. It is only passed down, from one cook to the next, by watching. That is what I love about it. Come and try it when you are here. Bye for now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, why food is the fastest way into a culture. Here is the thing. You can read every guidebook ever written, but a place is not really understood until it is tasted. Think about it. When a dish is put in front of you, a whole history comes with it. What is grown in that soil. What has been traded there for centuries. What is celebrated, and when. A staple is never just food. It is memory, pressed onto a plate. And the beauty is that you do not need perfect grammar to reach it. You just need to be curious. Ask how something is made. Ask what it is served with, and why. And almost always, the person will slow down, and smile, and tell you far more than a recipe. Because when you ask about the food, you are really asking about them.
